Hardhat - versions - Hardhat3 - ESM: Hardhat 3 embraces modern JavaScript by using ECMAScript Modules (ESM) by default. Configuration files must now use ESM, although CommonJS (CJS) modules are still supported in scripts and tests.
Hardhat - compiling - profiles: Build Profiles let you use different Solidity compiler configurations for different use cases. For example, you might use one Build Profile for running tests and another for deployment, each with settings optimized for that workflow.
Hardhat - compiling - profiles - define: To configure a Build Profile, use hardhat.config.ts
Hardhat - compiling - profiles - configu: You can use the --build-profile argument to choose which Build Profile to use when running Hardhat.
Hardhat - compiling - profiles - builtin: default: used by most tasks when you don’t specify a profile, with settings optimized for development speed and experience
production: recommended for deployments, with the optimizer and Isolated Builds enabled by default
Hardhat - deploying - Ignition: Hardhat Ignition is a declarative system for deploying smart contracts on Ethereum.
Hardhat - deploying - Ignition - feature: You define the desired final state (e.g., 'deploy Contract A, then call function B') in a module.
Idempotent. Running the same deployment command multiple times will not redeploy existing contracts; it will only execute new actions.
Hardhat - deploying - Ignition - modules: In Hardhat Ignition, deployments are defined through Ignition Modules.
You can create multiple modules in a single file, but each must have a unique ID. As a best practice, we suggest maintaining one module per file, naming the file after the module ID.
import { buildModule } from '@nomicfoundation/hardhat-ignition/modules'; export default buildModule('MyToken', (m) => { .... });
The m parameter being passed into the callback is an instance of a ModuleBuilder, which is an object with methods to define and configure your smart contract instances.
Before starting to run the deployment, Hardhat Ignition will create a deployment folder under ignition/deployments/. By default, the folder will be named chain-<chainId>
Ignition Modules can define Module Parameters and use them programmatically. ./ignition/parameters.json
Hardhat Ignition comes built-in with a visualize task that generates an HTML report
It’s possible to make changes to modules after having deployed them. Hardhat Ignition will continue from where it left off, and execute the new parts of the module.
Hardhat Ignition creates files for each deployment. The files are stored under the directory ./ignition/deployments/<DeploymentId>.
Hardhat Ignition makes this easy by allowing you to deploy your existing Ignition modules via a create2 deployment utilizing the CreateX factory.
When developing smart contracts, you may decide to use an upgradeable proxy pattern to allow for future upgrades to your contracts.
Hardhat Ignition supports deploying contracts using a Ledger hardware wallet via the Hardhat Ledger plugin.
Hardhat - verifying: Smart contract verification is used to prove that a deployed contract’s bytecode comes from a specific source code. This allows users and tools to inspect the original source on block explorers, like Etherscan and Blockscout, and verification services, like Sourcify.
Hardhat - verifying - hardhat-verify: If you initialized your project using hardhat --init and chose anything but the minimal project, you don’t need to do anything: hardhat-verify is already included as part of the template project.
Hardhat - verifying - hardhat-verify - c: The hardhat-verify plugin adds a verify object to the Hardhat configuration. With it, you can configure the different block explorers to verify your contracts on.
Hardhat - running: Hardhat also comes built-in with Hardhat Network, a local Ethereum network designed for development.
Hardhat - connecting - hardhat.config.ts: Configuring networks in hardhat.config.ts acts as the blueprint for your development workflow.
Run tasks with a simple flag (e.g., npx hardhat run script.js --network polygon). Hardhat automatically injects the correct RPC URL and private keys into the execution context.
Tools like @nomicfoundation/hardhat-verify require the network configuration to automatically submit your source code and ABI to explorers like Etherscan or Polygonscan.
You can simulate a live blockchain locally by adding a forking block to your network config. This allows you to test contracts against real, live mainnet data without spending gas.
Run your automated test suites (npx hardhat test --network sepolia) directly against testnets or custom staging environments to catch network-specific edge cases.
Keep your private keys and RPC URLs organized in one place using environment variables (process.env), preventing you from accidentally hardcoding credentials into multiple script files.
Most Hardhat ecosystem plugins (like gas reporters, deployment managers, and security analyzers) rely entirely on the configuration file to understand the target environment.
Hardhat - connecting - JsonRpcProvider: Yes, you can connect to blockchains not configured in your hardhat.config.ts. You can easily bypass the configuration file by instantiating an ethers.JsonRpcProvider directly in your JavaScript or TypeScript deployment and interaction scripts.
Foundry - components - forge: Forge: The heart of the framework. It's used for building, testing (including fuzz and invariant tests), and deploying smart contracts . Its Rust-based architecture makes it significantly faster than tools like Hardhat .
Foundry - components - cast: A command-line 'Swiss Army knife' for interacting with the EVM. You use it to send transactions, query blockchain data, and encode/decode data on the fly .
Foundry - components - anvil: Anvil: A fast local Ethereum node, similar to Hardhat Network, used for development and testing in an isolated environment . It can fork mainnet state for realistic testing .
Foundry - components - chisel: Chisel: A Solidity REPL (Read-Eval-Print Loop) that lets you quickly test and prototype Solidity code snippets in an interactive terminal .
Ethers - intro - definition: The ethers.js library aims to be a complete and compact library for interacting with the Ethereum Blockchain and its ecosystem.
Ethers - model - Provider: A Provider provides a connection to the blockchain, which can be used to query its current state, simulate execution and send transactions to update the state.
Ethers - model - Provider - Provider: A Provider is the primary method to interact with the read-only content on Ethereum.
Ethers - model - Contract - deploy() - i: You write a script that executes deployment steps one after another
Ethers - model - Contract - deploy() - n: Not idempotent. Running a script multiple times will typically attempt to redeploy everything, leading to duplicate contracts and wasted gas
Ethers - model - Wallet - Wallet: A Wallet manages a single private key which is used to sign transactions, messages and other common payloads.
Ethers - utilities - CryptographicFuncti: A fundamental building block of Ethereum is the underlying cryptographic primitives.
Viem - intro - definition: So we created viem: a TypeScript Interface for Ethereum that provides low-level stateless primitives for interacting with Ethereum.
Viem - intro - alternative: An alternative to ethers.js and web3.js with a focus on reliability, efficiency, and excellent developer experience.
Viem - intro - performance: In addition to the fast load times mentioned above, viem further tunes performance by only executing heavy asynchronous tasks when required and optimized encoding/parsing algorithms.
